one_line_takeaway: Machine unlearning methods that report near-zero forget accuracy mostly relocate the last-layer classifier weight for the forgotten class rather than erasing it from the feature representations, so a simple linear probe or nearest-class-center classifier on the frozen features can recover most of the 'forgotten' accuracy -- an illusion the authors partially close with a class-mean-features (CMF) classifier that forces the classifier to track the (still-informative) features.
key_finding: On CIFAR-100 forgetting 1 class, Random-Label achieves 0.00% output-level forget accuracy but 79.00% linear-probe forget accuracy and 87.20% NCC forget accuracy; SalUn similarly reaches 0.00% output forget accuracy but 77.80% linear-probe / 85.60% NCC forget accuracy. Even the Retain-only Retrain 'gold standard' -- which never saw the forgotten class -- yields 0.00% output forget accuracy yet 85.20% linear-probe forget accuracy on CIFAR-100 (1 class), because transferable representations still separate the unseen class. Classifier-only fine-tuning (updating just the last layer) matches or beats full-model unlearning at the output level (e.g., Classifier-only NegGrad+ on CIFAR-100/10-class: 66.85% retain / 0.00% forget vs. full-model 70.80% retain / 0.28% forget), demonstrating that output-level 'success' can be achieved by classifier reweighting alone, without altering the feature extractor. Applying the proposed CMF classifier substantially reduces feature-level leakage: e.g., UNSIR-with-CMF on CIFAR-10 (1 class) drops linear-probe forget accuracy to 31.16% (NCC 9.29%) versus UNSIR-without-CMF's 85.29% linear-probe forget accuracy, while retain accuracy stays comparable (91.63% vs. 89.71%). CMF-based methods no longer trivially reach 0% output-level forget accuracy (e.g., Random-Label+CMF has 80.70% output forget accuracy on CIFAR-10/1-class) -- the tradeoff is that CMF makes the classifier follow the (still partially informative) features rather than letting the classifier alone mask them.
core_contribution: The paper shows that state-of-the-art machine unlearning (MU) methods for class-forgetting in image classifiers achieve near-zero 'forget accuracy' only at the output level, while the underlying feature representations remain highly discriminative for the supposedly forgotten class. The authors trace this to a mechanism they call 'feature-classifier misalignment': unlearning methods mostly move the last-layer classifier weight vector for the forgotten class out of alignment with its class-mean feature (measured via the Neural Collapse NC3 metric), rather than removing information from the feature extractor itself. They formally prove (Proposition 1, under a Neural-Collapse assumption with fixed simplex-ETF class means) that NegGrad-style unlearning drives the forget-class weight to wun_k = -(1-gamma)*mu_k, which is sufficient to zero out output-level forget accuracy without touching the features, and confirm empirically that updating only the last-layer classifier ('classifier-only' fine-tuning) reproduces output-level unlearning performance comparable to full-model unlearning across NegGrad+, Random-Label, SalUn, SVD, SCRUB and UNSIR. Motivated by this diagnosis, they propose a Class-Mean-Features (CMF) classifier -- replacing the trainable last-layer weights with the running class-mean feature vectors (a technique adapted from Jiang et al., 2024, originally proposed for parameter reduction, not unlearning) -- and plug it into five existing MU methods (Random-Label, SalUn, NegGrad+, SCRUB, UNSIR) to force forgetting to occur in the representation space too.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker-vs-defender threat model. The paper's concern is an evaluation-validity problem: a model owner (or auditor) applies a machine unlearning method to satisfy privacy/right-to-be-forgotten or copyright-removal requirements and validates success using standard output-level forget/retain accuracy. The paper shows this validation can be an illusion -- forgotten-class information survives in hidden representations and can be recovered by simply training a new linear probe (or applying nearest-class-center classification) on the frozen features, with no access to model weights beyond the feature extractor and no retraining of the backbone. This is a passive representation-inversion-style risk (an adversary/auditor with only feature access can recover 'forgotten' class information), not an active poisoning, evasion, or extraction attack in the conventional ML-security sense.
limitations: Restricted to class-unlearning in image classification (ResNet/ViT on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet); the paper explicitly does not test generative diffusion models or large language models, though it notes the phenomenon may parallel known LLM/diffusion unlearning fragility (citing Suriyakumar et al., 2024 for concept re-emergence after fine-tuning) as future work. The core theoretical result (Proposition 1) relies on an idealized Neural Collapse assumption (fixed simplex-ETF class means that do not shift during unlearning), which is an approximation of real training dynamics. CMF itself does not eliminate feature-level leakage entirely -- Table 3 numbers show CMF-based methods still retain non-trivial forget accuracy under linear probing (e.g., Scrub+CMF on Tiny-ImageNet/20-class: 54.28% linear-probe forget accuracy), and CMF substantially raises output-level forget accuracy, meaning practitioners who rely solely on output metrics would see CMF-based methods as 'worse' unlearning. The paper does not evaluate MIA (membership inference) as a metric, explicitly arguing it is not relevant to their class-forgetting setting rather than testing it and finding it inadequate.

datasets_used: ViT-S/16 variance results in Appendix D (not fully extracted here)
quantitative_results: CIFAR-100, 1-class forget, Random-Label: 0.00% output forget accuracy vs 79.00% linear-probe forget accuracy vs 87.20% NCC forget accuracy
CIFAR-100, 1-class forget, SalUn: 0.00% output forget accuracy vs 77.80% linear-probe forget accuracy vs 85.60% NCC forget accuracy
CIFAR-100, 1-class forget, Retain-only Retrain (gold standard): 0.00% output forget accuracy vs 85.20% linear-probe forget accuracy vs 70.40% NCC forget accuracy
CIFAR-100, 10-class forget, Classifier-only NegGrad+: 66.85% retain / 0.00% forget (output) vs Full-model NegGrad+: 70.80% retain / 0.28% forget (output)
CIFAR-10, 1-class forget, UNSIR: 85.29% linear-probe forget accuracy without CMF vs 31.16% linear-probe forget accuracy with CMF (retain accuracy 89.71% vs 91.63%)
CIFAR-10, 1-class forget, Random-Label+CMF: output forget accuracy rises to 80.70% (vs 0.00% without CMF), illustrating the output/feature tradeoff CMF introduces
baselines_compared: Retain-only Retrain (train from scratch on retain set)
each of NegGrad+/Random-Label/SalUn/SCRUB/UNSIR combined with the proposed CMF classifier
